summary:
President Richard Nixon's visit to the People's Republic of China in February 1972 was one of the most consequential diplomatic openings of the Cold War era, ending over two decades of US non-recognition of the Communist government in Beijing. The visit was made possible by secret preparatory work by National Security Advisor Henry Kissinger, who visited Beijing in July 1971 to arrange the groundwork. Nixon met with Mao Zedong and Premier Zhou Enlai, and the visit produced the Shanghai Communiqué, in which the US acknowledged that all Chinese on both sides of the Taiwan Strait maintained that there was one China and that Taiwan was part of China.

The Nixon-Mao rapprochement was driven by shared strategic interests, particularly the mutual concern about Soviet power. By opening to China, the United States exploited the [[event:3352|Sino-Soviet Split]] to create a triangular diplomatic framework that increased pressure on the Soviet Union. China gained de facto international legitimacy and a counterbalance to Soviet pressure. The opening transformed Asian geopolitics and ultimately enabled the normalization of US-China relations in 1979 under President Carter, which in turn facilitated China's subsequent economic integration into the global order.

figures.summary: American diplomatic overture to the PRC
